args_redacted.query: zero trust architecture NIST principles never trust always verify
defense in depth layered security strategy explained

args_redacted.raw:
{"path": "workspace/security_comparison.txt", "content": "================================================================================
  COMPARISON: ZERO TRUST SECURITY vs. DEFENSE IN DEPTH
================================================================================

1. OVERVIEW
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

ZERO TRUST SECURITY
  A security model centered on the principle of "never trust, always verify."
  It assumes that threats exist both outside and inside the traditional network
  perimeter, and therefore no user, device, or application should be trusted by
  default — even if they are already inside the network. Every access request
  must be authenticated, authorized, and continuously validated.

DEFENSE IN DEPTH
  A layered security strategy that employs multiple, overlapping defensive
  controls to protect information assets. The idea is that if one layer fails,
  the next layer will catch the threat. It is inspired by military strategy
  and applies controls across physical, technical, and administrative domains.


2. CORE PRINCIPLES
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

ZERO TRUST PRINCIPLES:
  • Never Trust, Always Verify — Every access request is treated as if it
    originates from an untrusted network.
  • Least Privilege Access — Users and systems are granted only the minimum
    level of access needed to perform their function.
  • Micro-Segmentation — Networks are divided into small, isolated zones;
    lateral movement is severely restricted.
  • Continuous Monitoring & Validation — Sessions and access rights are
    continuously inspected and re-validated; trust is never permanent.
  • Assume Breach — Operate as if the network is already compromised; design
    systems to minimize blast radius.
  • Explicit Verification — Use multiple signals (identity, device health,
    location, behavior) to make access decisions.

DEFENSE IN DEPTH PRINCIPLES:
  • Layered Controls — Deploy multiple independent security controls so that
    no single failure leads to a complete compromise.
  • Physical Security — Guards, locks, cameras, biometric scanners, secure
    facilities.
  • Perimeter Security — Firewalls, IDS/IPS, DMZs, VPN gateways.
  • Network Security — Network segmentation, VLANs, encrypted tunnels, NAC.
  • Host Security — Endpoint protection (EDR/antivirus), host firewalls,
    patch management, hardened configurations.
  • Application Security — Secure coding, WAFs, input validation, SAST/DAST.
  • Data Security — Encryption at rest and in transit, DLP, backups, access
    controls.
  • Administrative Controls — Policies, training, incident response plans,
    audits, and compliance frameworks.


3. KEY DIFFERENCES
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

┌──────────────────────┬─────────────────────────────┬──────────────────────────────┐
│ ASPECT               │ ZERO TRUST                  │ DEFENSE IN DEPTH             │
├──────────────────────┼─────────────────────────────┼──────────────────────────────┤
│                      │                             │                              │
│ Philosophy           │ Trust is a vulnerability;   │ Redundancy prevents failure; │
│                      │ eliminate implicit trust    │ no single layer is enough    │
│                      │                             │                              │
│ Network Assumption   │ The network is always       │ The perimeter can be         │
│                      │ hostile; perimeter is dead  │ defended and hardened        │
│                      │                             │                              │
│ Focus                │ Identity-centric; who you   │ Asset-centric; protecting    │
│                      │ are matters more than where │ the crown jewels through     │
│                      │ you are connecting from     │ multiple barriers            │
│                      │                             │                              │
│ Access Control       │ Dynamic, risk-based,        │ Often static, role-based,    │
│                      │ session-level decisions     │ and perimeter-focused        │
│                      │                             │                              │
│ Lateral Movement     │ Actively prevented via      │ Slowed but not always        │
│                      │ micro-segmentation          │ eliminated                   │
│                      │                             │                              │
│ Trust Model          │ No implicit trust; every    │ Some implicit trust inside   │
│                      │ access is verified          │ the perimeter                │
│                      │                             │                              │
│ Primary Technologies │ IAM, MFA, ZTNA, SDP,        │ Firewalls, IDS/IPS, AV,      │
│                      │ micro-segmentation, CASB    │ WAF, SIEM, DLP, VPN          │
│                      │                             │                              │
│ Response to

Focus                | Identity-centric; who you   | Asset-centric; protecting
                     | are matters more than where | the crown jewels through
                     | you are connecting from     | multiple barriers
---------------------|-----------------------------|-----------------------------
Access Control       | Dynamic, risk-based,        | Often static, role-based,
                     | session-level decisions     | and perimeter-focused
---------------------|-----------------------------|-----------------------------
Lateral Movement     | Actively prevented via      | Slowed but not always
                     | micro-segmentation          | eliminated
---------------------|-----------------------------|-----------------------------
Trust Model          | No implicit trust; every    | Some implicit trust inside
                     | access is verified          | the perimeter
---------------------|-----------------------------|-----------------------------
Primary Technologies | IAM, MFA, ZTNA, SDP,        | Firewalls, IDS/IPS, AV,
                     | micro-segmentation, CASB    | WAF, SIEM, DLP, VPN
---------------------|-----------------------------|-----------------------------
Response to Breach   | Contain blast radius;       | Detect and block at the
                     | assume breach is ongoing    | next layer; layered defense
---------------------|-----------------------------|-----------------------------
Origin                | Modern (circa 2010,         | Ancient military concept;
                     | popularized by For
